questions.q: What is the fundamental difference between semantic segmentation and instance segmentation?
options: Semantic is faster; instance is more accurate
Semantic assigns class labels to pixels; instance additionally distinguishes between separate objects of the same class
Instance works only on binary images
Semantic and instance are the same method
questions.explanation: Semantic segmentation labels each pixel with a class (e.g., 'car', 'road'). Instance segmentation further identifies individual object instances, so two separate cars receive different IDs.

questions.q: What does a segmentation mask represent?
options: A filter applied to reduce image noise
A binary or multi-class map where each pixel value indicates its category or instance ID
A bounding box around objects
A set of image coordinates
questions.explanation: A segmentation mask is an image-sized label map where each pixel encodes semantic class or instance identity. Binary masks show presence/absence; multi-class masks show categorical assignments.

questions.q: Why is the U-Net architecture effective for image segmentation?
options: It uses only fully connected layers
It combines downsampling (encoder) and upsampling (decoder) with skip connections, enabling precise localization of fine details
It requires less training data than other methods
It cannot process large images
questions.explanation: U-Net's encoder contracts spatial information while capturing context; the decoder upsamples while skip connections preserve fine-grained details from the encoder. This combination excels at precise pixel-level prediction.

questions.q: What is the purpose of skip connections in U-Net?
options: To reduce computation time
To concatenate features from encoder layers to corresponding decoder layers, preserving spatial information and fine details
To randomly shuffle feature maps
To increase model parameters significantly
questions.explanation: Skip connections fuse high-resolution encoder features with coarse decoder features, allowing the decoder to recover fine structural details. This addresses the information loss from aggressive downsampling.

questions.q: How does pixel-level classification differ from image-level classification?
options: Pixel-level is less accurate
Pixel-level assigns a class label to every individual pixel; image-level assigns a single label to the entire image
They are identical methods
Pixel-level cannot use neural networks
questions.explanation: Image classification produces one label per image. Pixel-level classification (segmentation) produces a label for each pixel, enabling detailed spatial understanding and object boundary precision.

questions.q: What is Dice loss and when is it particularly useful?
options: A loss function that measures image brightness
A loss function that directly optimizes the Dice coefficient (F1-score), effective for imbalanced segmentation tasks where background overwhelms foreground
A form of data augmentation
A regularization technique to prevent overfitting
questions.explanation: Dice loss ∝ 1 - (2TP)/(2TP + FP + FN) treats class imbalance by focusing on overlap rather than pixel accuracy. It's crucial when segmenting small objects against large backgrounds.

questions.q: How does instance segmentation incorporate object detection concepts?
options: It ignores detection entirely
It first detects objects via bounding boxes or region proposals, then performs segmentation within each region, combining localization and pixel-level masks
It only labels entire images
Instance segmentation predates object detection
questions.explanation: Instance segmentation methods like Mask R-CNN extend object detection by predicting a segmentation mask for each detected instance, combining bounding box localization with precise pixel-level boundaries.

questions.q: What challenge does class imbalance present in semantic segmentation?
options: All pixels are equally frequent in images
Small classes (e.g., road signs) occupy far fewer pixels than background, causing models to bias toward majority classes and ignore minorities
Models automatically balance classes
Class imbalance is irrelevant to segmentation
questions.explanation: Segmentation datasets often have severe class imbalance: sky/background overwhelm foreground objects. Models trained with standard loss functions ignore minority classes; weighted loss functions or Dice loss help.

questions.q: What does encoder-decoder architecture in segmentation models do?
options: It only encodes images without decoding
The encoder downsamples to extract features; the decoder upsamples to produce pixel-level predictions, reversing spatial reduction while leveraging learned features
It processes images linearly without spatial hierarchy
Both encoder and decoder have identical architectures
questions.explanation: Encoder-decoder is fundamental to segmentation. The encoder creates a compact feature representation; the decoder reconstructs spatial detail for dense predictions, balancing efficiency and precision.

questions.q: Why is IoU a common metric for evaluating segmentation models?
options: It measures inference speed
It computes intersection over union between predicted masks and ground truth, quantifying spatial overlap accuracy for each class
It only works for binary segmentation
IoU cannot evaluate instance segmentation
questions.explanation: Segmentation IoU (or Jaccard Index) measures pixel-level overlap accuracy. Mean IoU across all classes is the standard benchmark. Higher IoU indicates better spatial prediction accuracy.
